blocks.text: keep my commandments and live, keep my teachings as the apple of your eye;
2 : The or pupil, the center and means of vision, a metaphor for what one loves and protects (e.g., Deut 32.10; Ps 17.8).
bind them on your fingers, write them on the tablet of your heart.
3 : On teaching as adornment, see 1.9n.
Say to wisdom, “You are my sister,” and call insight your intimate friend,
4 : , a term of endearment for a female lover in ancient Near Eastern love poetry (e.g., Song 4.9–10,12; 5.1–2).
that they may keep you from the loose woman, from the adulteress with her smooth words.
For at the window of my house I looked out through my lattice,
6 : Peering through a with expectation or curiosity (Gen 26.8; Judg 5.28; 2 Sam 6.16; 2 Kings 9.30–32).
and I saw among the simple ones, I observed among the youths, a young man without sense,
passing along the street near her corner, taking the road to her house
in the twilight, in the evening, at the time of night and darkness.
8–9 : , when people still move about the city. , when people, including lovers, can move discreetly and conceal themselves (e.g., Gen 30.15–17; Song 3.1–4; Job 24.15).
Then a woman comes toward him, decked out like a prostitute, wily of heart.
10 : , perhaps she covers her face with a veil (Gen 38.14–15; cf. Job 24.15).
She is loud and wayward; her feet do not stay at home;
11 : , see 9.13. , perhaps a play on words, since is a euphemism for genitalia (e.g., Deut 28.57; Ezek 16.25).
now in the street, now in the squares, and at every corner she lies in wait.
12 : , the woman and Wisdom frequent the same places; see 1.20–21; 8.2–3; 9.3,14.
She seizes him and kisses him, and with impudent face she says to him:
“I had to offer sacrifices, and today I have paid my vows;

14 : suggests she is pious and honest (e.g., Pss 50.14; 66.13; Isa 19.21; Jon 2.9). Sacrifices of well-being (so Hebrew, cf. Lev 7.15–17; 19.5) implies she has fresh meat for dinner, a rarity (see 9.2n.); to eat with her helps her to fulfill her religious obligation.
so now I have come out to meet you, to seek you eagerly, and I have found you!
I have decked my couch with coverings, colored spreads of Egyptian linen;
I have perfumed my bed with myrrh, aloes, and cinnamon.

16–17 : Her is thick with luxurious fabrics and sensual aromas: from Egypt (e.g., Isa 3.23; Ezek 16.10,13); , an aromatic resin (Ps 45.9; Song 1.13; 4.14; 5.5,13); from India; and from Sri Lanka (Ex 30.23; Song 4.14).
Come, let us take our fill of love until morning; let us delight ourselves with love.
18 : , eating and drinking are metaphors for sex (cf. 9.17; 30.20; Song 5.1).
For my husband is not at home; he has gone on a long journey.
He took a bag of money with him; he will not come home until full moon.”
14–20 : Her invitation.
With much seductive speech she persuades him; with her smooth talk she compels him.
Right away he follows her, and goes like an ox to the slaughter, or bounds like a stag toward the trap
until an arrow pierces its entrails. He is like a bird rushing into a snare, not knowing that it will cost him his life.
And now, my children, listen to me, and be attentive to the words of my mouth.
Do not let your hearts turn aside to her ways; do not stray into her paths.
For many are those she has laid low, and numerous are her victims.
Her house is the way to Sheol, going down to the chambers of death.
1.1–9.18: The proverbs of Solomon son of David, king of Israel.
7.1–27: Another warning about the “strange” woman (see 2.16–22; 5.1–23; 6.20–35).
